Cloth diapers are a wonderful option that won’t take up space in a landfill. Smart Bottoms diaper’s hemp and organic cotton interior reduces the risk of rash for babies with sensitive skin. A series of buttons allow you to adjust the diapers size, accommodating children between 10 and 35 pounds, so you don’t have to buy new ones as your baby grows.
Parent Reviews
"I used newborn and one size smart bottoms. I love them. My baby was 9lbs to start so I cant really say how well they would fit small babies but I believe I started on the second rise so they might be okay." — zooeyc
If reusable diapers aren’t for you but you want to reduce waste, Dyper’s Bamboo Diapers could be a good alternative. Made with Bamboo Viscose for maximum absorbency, the plant-based diapers are free from a myriad of chemicals including chlorine, latex, alcohol, lotions and phthalates. This is ideal for children with sensitive skin. For an added eco-friendly element, and for an additional $50 a month, you can enroll in the REDYPER service, which will send soiled diapers back to the company for them to compost.
Parent Reviews
"dyper is working great for us now. The absorb well, hold lots of pee." — FTMbabyboy2016
"We really like the Dyper brand. My LO has chunky legs and they’re great for him. But I think they’re similar in shape to Pampers." —kel2021
Made from 100 percent vegan- and PETA-certified materials, this crossbody tote is an organizer’s dream. The wide main compartment features four elasticized interior pockets to sort and store all your diapering gear, as well as a wallet pouch to hold other essential items. The waterproof bag comes with two stroller straps and a crossbody strap to allow for multiple carrying options.
Parent Reviews
"Just started to use this diaper bag. The color is a deep and beautiful gray, and the fabric is nice and soft. The wallet inside is a great feature. I love the many compartments as well, and the compartments that are rubberized, without a zipper. Definitely recommend this."
A trusty diaper ointment can prevent your baby’s bottom from getting red and chapped, and soothe their tush when the inevitable rash arrives. Free of petroleum, parabens or artificial fragrance, Earth Mama’s balm is made from 100 percent organic ingredients, such as shea butter, beeswax, and calendula extract.
Parent Reviews
"Earth Mama is my favorite brand by far. Their nipple butter, diaper cream, lotion, bubble bath…it’s all organic and all that stuff and smells like heaven. But more importantly it’s helped my son through eczema and any other skin issues he’s had. It’s pricier but worth it imo." — thelonecoconut
"Earth mama diaper balm is so gentle and easily prevented/healed diaper rashes!" — akayle91
Baby wipes are something you’ll use countless times during the course of a given day, so it’s only natural that you’d want to opt for a brand that’s eco-friendly. Caboo’s wipes are made from bamboo, a renewable resource that can be replenished faster than other plants and requires less energy to turn into products. They’re free from potentially harmful ingredients, including alcohol, parabens, chlorine, formaldehyde, phthalates and more.
Parent Reviews
"I love these wipes! They aren't flimsy at all so I don't have to worry about my fingers going through them. They're wet but not soaked and not too dry. I love that they have texture so they pick up stuff and don't just spread it around. I also feel good about using them because of how eco-friendly they are!"
"I use these wipes for EVERYTHING. Aside from typical baby wipe use I also keep these in my kitchen to clean up my kids after eating. These are gentle enough that I can wipe food off my toddlers face without bothering skin. I can also wipe up food/sticky messes with these wipes and they hold up great!"
A play gym can help foster your child’s growth and development, and Skip Hop’s is a popular choice among parents for a myriad of reasons: the cushy material, the engaging toys, and the tummy time pillow, to name a few. But eco-conscious parents will appreciate the fact that this is free from phthalates, a harmful chemical that is often found in plastic toys.
Parent Reviews
"When my daughter was an infant we lived in a very tiny house. The activity mat for us was a must have.we used it daily, and the colors and dangly toys really grabbed her interest. That is where she first learned to reach and also roll over. They fold fairly flat, so when not in use I stored under my couch or slid it behind the couch."
"They fold up and hide away easily. I found the activity gym important for development. It was where DS did 'tummy time' and where he learned to roll over."
Moms of young babies swear by the no-frills pacifier. Made out of rubber from the sustainable Hevea Brasiliensis trees, Natursutten’s pacifiers are BPA-free. They also have an orthodontic design that is said to support the development of your baby’s teeth and jaw.
Parent Reviews
"I use Natursutten. They are a great company and really stand by their product."
"i use natural rubber natursutten/goldi and love it!"
DaVici’s convertible crib grows with your baby, so it won’t waste away in a landfill after a year of use. The 4-in-1 sleep space transitions from a crib, to a toddler bed, to a daybed and, finally, a twin bed. Made from 100 percent sustainable New Zealand pine, the crib is GREENGUARD Gold certified, which means it’s free from a laundry list of potentially harmful chemicals.
Parent Reviews
"I have the Davinci Kalani 4-in-1 Convertible Crib and have been very happy with it. He is 3 and is still sleeping in the crib. We also just ordered the DaVinci Sleigh Toddler Bed to start transitioning him to for when baby arrives." — smileitsbabytime
Green Sprouts sells a wide range of sustainably-made products, including muslin burp cloths. Made from 100 percent cotton, the cloths’ four absorbent layers are thick enough to handle all of your baby’s drool and spit-up but breathable enough that baby will be cozy. Machine washable, they stay super soft no matter how many times you run them through.
Parent Reviews
"My favorite are these Green Sprouts muslin burp cloths. They come in a 3 pack and they have a lot of coverage and really are absorbent!!" —kcmom2019
"I really like these burp cloths by green sprouts. Several washes so far and no shrinking. Holds a good bit of spit up too!" — kksho
Avanchy’s feeding set is sure to come in handy once you start serving up solids. It’s made from bamboo, which is a renewable resource1, and food-grade silicone, which is soft and easy to clean. The set is free from plastic, BPA and PVC, and designed to last—you can expect to be reusing this set with future kiddos.
Parent Reviews
"We use the Avanchy baby plates and bowls which are made of bamboo and have suction. We got rid of the EZPZ silicone plates because they constantly tasted like soap and made food taste like soap. I even tried soaking them in baking soda and vinegar or using fragrance free dish soap. Never got the taste out. The bamboo dishware is much better. Got 2 of those as well." — ajas17
This features a viscose cover that’s naturally chemical free, along with a woven air core that’s 90 percent air and 10 percent food-grade polymer. This mattress is also GREENGUARD Gold certified, so it’s quality tested and certified for low emissions.
Parent Reviews
"We have it. It’s worth it for me. I didn’t really splurge on anything else and told my husband if I was going to, it was going to be the mattress.A huge plus is that it’s sooo easy to clean. I want to get another for this baby, just waiting for another sale."
Glass bottles are naturally free from toxic chemicals, giving eco-friendly parents one less thing to worry about. Philips Avent’s bottles are made from heat- and thermal shock-resistant borosilicate glass—a fancy way of saying they can go from the fridge to a container of warm water without fear of breaking. The bottles’ nipples feature an airflex valve to control air intake, which reduces colic and discomfort.
Parent Reviews
"We really like the Philips Avent glass bottle that we had. I think it was 5 oz, so it was small and not heavy, and we never had any issues with it!" —erilapp
This eco-conscious and sustainable brand makes beautiful organic cotton clothing, bedding, playtime accessories and more. Made in India, everything in this line is Global Organic Textile Standard (GOTS)-certified, meaning it’s made from at least 70 percent organic fibers, free of harmful chemicals, and made in safe and ethical working conditions. The wide assortment of styles are available in sizes from newborn to 4T.
Parent Reviews
"First time buyer of this brand. It was more expensive than some of the clothing/pajamas I buy for her but I fell in love with the rainbow print for my one year old rainbow baby girl. When I got these I was so impressed with the quality of the material! They are SO SOFT. They are stretchy. They are just wonderful pajamas and you can feel the quality. My only frustration is that these don't come in my size because I would wear these every day! If you're on the fence, buy them. You won't regret it!"
One of the most classic types of baby toys, stackers have stood the test of time for good reason—they boost your baby’s fine and gross motor skills and also bolster hand-eye coordination. Green Toy’s version—a set of six cups with labeled numbers—is made from 100 percent recycled plastic milk containers. They also meet the FDA food contact standards and are dishwasher safe.
Parent Reviews
"I have had this now for about 7 years and 3 kids! I love that it is eco friendly and non toxic, but besides that- its just a great durable toy. The colors stay, has not cracked, and is also easy to clean."
"I love this stacker because its made responsibly, in the USA! Woohoo! I also love that the colors aren't super garish like so many baby toys out there! Yes, its colorful but not annoyingly so!"
Not only are these little booties soft and comfortable on your baby’s feet, but they actually stay on, unlike pretty much every other infant shoe ever designed. They’re also Global Organic Textile Standard (GOTS)-certified, meaning they’re made from at least 70 percent organic fibers, free of harmful chemicals, and made in safe and ethical working conditions. So you can feel good knowing they're made with sustainable materials.
Parent Reviews
"Zutano booties are my favorite! The zutano just fit tighter and more secure and seem warmer than the other booties I’ve tried. And they stay on newborn feet!" —hellomello44
"Get a pair of Zutanos! They are pricey but they snap on and NEVER come off! No matter what size your baby's foot is, big or small, they will somehow kick off socks. I despise constantly putting socks back on. My first was born in October and we live in WI and I found these. They are amazing :-)" — babylaporte
Not only do parents love the softness and durability of these crib sheets from Burt’s Bees, but they also appreciate the fact that they’re made cotton that’s 100 percent organic and Global Organic Textile Standard (GOTS)-certified, meaning it’s made from at least 70 percent organic fibers, free of harmful chemicals, and made in safe and ethical working conditions. Parents say they’re gentle on baby’s skin and don’t pill in the wash.
Parent Reviews
"I’ve always bought Burt’s Bee’s but I was just gifted these ones and they are so incredibly soft and great quality!"
"Burts bees are my favorite sheets and last a long time."
You can get a lot of mileage out of this mini crib. Compared to DaVinci’s full-sized crib (57.50 inches by 30.75 inches), the mini (40 inches by 28.785 inches) takes up significantly less floor space, so you won’t bump into it as you try to sneak out of their room. Made of durable New Zealand pinewood, the DaVinci convertible crib features four adjustable mattress positions to accommodate your baby as they start to sit, stand and become more mobile. Once your child is older, you can purchase a conversion kit to turn it into a mini toddler bed, daybed or twin-size bed. DaVinci’s mini crib is available in five different finishes, so it’s easy to find one that works with your aesthetic. As a bonus, the crib is Greenguard Gold certified, which means your baby isn’t sleeping in something that’s giving off a lot of volatile chemicals.
Parent Reviews
"This is a really great little crib for small spaces. Very sturdy and good quality wood/finish."
"It’s very light weight and easy to move from room to room."
Not only do traditional high chairs take up a ton of space, but they are easy to trip over while navigating around your dining space. The Inglesina's Fast Table Chair takes care of both these problems by hooking onto your table. Designed for babies and toddlers up to 37 pounds, the high chair is compatible with tables or countertops up to 3.5 inches thick and attaches with easy-to-use couplings. Its portable, collapsible design makes it easy to move from the kitchen to the dining room or take on trips. Just note that it should never be attached to a glass table, a flimsy surface or a pedestal table with one central support.
Parent Reviews
"The Inglesina chair is one of my favorite baby purchases ever. [My son] eats a lot in his high chair but we have it hooked onto our bar and he eats snacks there while I cook or clean the kitchen... It screws onto anything with a flat surface. Most restaurants it worked with, but not if the table at grooves or wobbly. It has to be super sturdy for you to hook it on to."
"It attaches to your counter and I love it!" —D1222
With a 31-by-15.5 inch base, the BabyBjörn Bouncer Bliss takes up minimal floor space when in use. It’s light enough to move between rooms so you always have a place for your child to hang out, and easily folds flat so you can store in the closet or against a wall when not in use. At $250, the bouncer is a splurge—but a worthwhile one, as it grows with your child. You can use it as a bouncer until they weigh 20 pounds or learn to sit upright. Once they hit that milestone, turn the fabric seat around for a comfy children’s chair.
Parent Reviews
"I like that it easily folds down so I will move it into our bedroom while I shower or need to put away laundry and it keeps her occupied for a bit." —firewalk
"My baby loves it, and there is a bar with toys that you can buy as an attachment. Baby loves that as well!" —Katelyn1214
Fisher-Price’s plush playmat gives your baby gentle padding while they work on their gross and fine motor skills. It features 20 different "sensory discoveries," including a light-up musical otter, scented cloud, leaf teether and mirror for your baby to explore. At around 34 by 32 inches, it doesn’t take up too much real estate, but it’s also easy to store when it’s not in use. Simply unhook the poles for a flat mat that can be placed under a bed or in a closet.
Parent Reviews
"Easy to put together, and easy to clean. Cute design. Baby will lay on this mat for a while looking and swatting at all the toys."
"This was so great for my daughter, she loved it. She can stay there for about an hour just looking at the toys that hang. The fabric is soft and it’s a great size. It’s easy to fold and put away."
With the help of magnets, the Puj Flyte folds into a tub that fits in most sinks while keeping your baby in the appropriate cradle position. The tub’s mold- and mildew-resistant foam is sturdy enough to support children ages 0 to 6 months. The cutouts on the side prevent water overflow, while the grippy texture prevents slipping. Once you’re done, unfold the tub for a flat mat that will dry in seconds when hung on a door.
Parent Reviews
"Puj tub forever. My very favorite baby item. We’re on our third kiddo, same tub, no mold, easy to store." —kfpete
"This thing is aaaamazing. I love that it folds flat. It dries so fast, and my daughter was really comfortable in it. We used it up until she was 6 months old and just about popping out of it."
This JPMA-certified changing table sits on wheels, allowing you to shift it around your room and home as needed. But don’t worry—two locking wheels keep it from moving when it’s time to change the baby's diapers. An easy-to-clean vinyl waterproof changing pad rests on top, while two roomy lower shelves provide plenty of storage for changing supplies and other essential gear. You might even be able to use it as a dresser, depending on how many clothes your little one owns.
Parent Reviews
"What a FANTASTIC product for the price! It's not easy finding a high-quality changing table that can easily be moved around."
The Dekor Mini is about the size of a standard bathroom garbage can, giving it a smaller footprint than most diaper pails. Because of its petite size, it can easily fit into corners, under counters or in other small spaces in your home. The pail features triple odor control and a non-absorbent plastic casing to keep your place from getting stinky. It even features a quiet-close lid so it won’t startle or wake your baby.
Parent Reviews
"I love my Dekor and replacement bags are super cheap. Never had an issue with smells. used if for almost 2 years with my first and definitely using it with my second."—Polkadotz86
Bottles and pump parts can be tricky to clean, so you really need special brushes to get into all the nooks and crannies. While most bottle cleaning sets take up valuable counter space, Boon’s set provides four different brushes in a compact storage container — less than 2 by 2 inches. The brushes all have durable nylon bristles that won’t bend or break with repeat use, and the vase has a drain that helps brushes dry properly. Bonus: the set has a cute caci design, so it doubles as decor.
Parent Reviews
"I LOVE this bottle brush set. Also super cute." —Elehim
"Works well with a variety of bottles and sippy cups. I especially like the straw brush. The handy cup that it comes in is super cute yet very functional with allowing all the brushes to properly dry."
Your walls are a great place to utilize space when you’re dealing with limited square footage. These shelves can be easily mounted just about anywhere. At four inches wide, they have a large enough depth for several books, teething toys, and small stuffed animals. A bar across the center helps prevent items from toppling over.
Parent Reviews
"Absolutely love this! It holds up plenty of books and it’s sturdy. I don’t have to worry about it falling down."
"This little bookshelf was exactly what I was looking for! Sturdy and price is good too!"
Having a baby means adding more cleansers, lotions and toys to your bathroom. The Tub Cubby hangs on your shower wall with the help of suction hooks, so your baby’s gear stays organized and in one place. Featuring four pockets to store all of your baby’s bathtime needs, the Tub Cubby is crafted from mesh to let excess water drain out for proper drying. It also comes with additional hooks to hang loofahs or brushes.
Parent Reviews
"Not only is there plenty of room for six rubber animals, complete foam alphabet and 10-digit numbers, and other random toys, this organizer actually sticks to the wall! …Also, I love this toy bag because it leaves corner tub space available for shampoo, conditioner, and soap bottles. In my house, that extra space is priceless! Don't look any further than this product. It is AWESOME."
"It holds a variety of toys and has held up to everyone pulling on it, including my not so gentle toddlers! This thing doesn't budge and all of our toys get to air dry instead of becoming gross in the tub or extra bin."
Every little bit of storage counts when you have a small space, and over-the-door organizers are a great way to utilize an often unused area. The Vesta Baby organizer has four oversized storage pockets to hold diapers, wipes, toys and more, as well as two smaller compartments to stash on-hand items like pacifiers, thermometers or combs. Plus, each pocket has a clear window to make it easy to find what you need. A sturdy back and thick inserts maintain the organizer’s shape and prevent it from bending.
Parent Reviews
"Our baby rooms in our bedroom, and between our furniture and his, we had very limited space and his dresser/changing table is only 3 drawers. This caddy holds muslin blankets, burp clothes, medicine, lotion, you name it! Love it."
"This works really well in our daughter’s nursery. It was easy to set up and hang. It holds plenty and weight has not been an issue."
Between creams, wipes and diapers, you need a lot of gear to keep your baby’s bottom clean. Maliton’s caddy makes it easy to store your diapering essentials so you can quickly access them when needed. It features long handles so you can tote it from room to room, as well as a removable divider so you can create compartments that suit your needs. Made of durable cotton rope for a soft-but-sturdy feel, the caddy is pretty enough to work with any aesthetic. Once your baby is out of diapers, you can use it to store pretty much anything.
Parent Reviews
"I really like the look of this basket and I like how the handles stand up to make it easy to grab when needed."
"We use this daily in our house! It’s functional and attractive, which is saying a lot considering it’s a diaper caddy."
Stashing baby’s bottles, pacifiers and pump parts in your drying rack along with your regular dishes raises the risk they’ll get damaged or soiled. So, having a dedicated drying rack is a must. The OXO Tot Space Saving Drying Rack has a vertical design to help make good use of wall space—not suck up valuable counter space. Enjoy nine different prongs for bottle parts and toys, while a top compartment is great for drying lids and other small items.
Parent Reviews
"Love this drying rack! Takes up so much less space than other brands."
"This thing is amazing. Very sturdy and has so much room to hang pump parts, bottles, pacifiers, teethers, and more. Love it!"
Technically Aden + Anais Swaddle Blankets are for, well, swaddling. But you can also use them as burp cloths and breathable nursing covers, eliminating the need for other items. Available in a slew of pretty patterns, the soft muslin blanket can be rolled up to fit into a basket or drawer for easy, space-saving storage.
Parent Reviews
"I absolutely love my Aden + Anais swaddles. I bought 6 with my son and got a few more as hand-me-downs for this baby. They’re amazingly soft and just great quality."—hillarya92
"They are the best. They last the longest too and get softer each time you wash." —kpnumber3
Rocking chairs and gliders can be behemoths in certain spaces, but the Delta Children Blair Slim Nursery Glider is on the more petite side. This rocking chair is a little over 2 feet wide, so you can easily tuck it into a corner or other small space. The chair’s woven fabric is easy to clean (a handy feature in case of spit-ups) and thick padded arms help support you while you rock. A 360-degree swivel feature allows you to move around without actually having to get up.
Parent Reviews
"I got this glider, swivel, rocker from Delta Children. I have been sitting on it last week. So comfy!" - mishjavier
This JPMA-certified playard provides a place for your baby to play or sleep. It is designed for children up to 30 pounds and 35 inches tall, so you can get a few years of use from it. The 3-by-3 foot playard has safari designs on the side and mattress, making for a cheery decor. When it’s not in use, it can be folded and stored in a compact travel bag.
Parent Reviews
"Definitely one of my favorite purchases…Very lightweight, awesome size, she loves rolling around in it. Would definitely recommend!!"
"Baby spends a lot of time playing, contemplating, and cracking himself up in the playard. It is extra wide. We keep it in our living room, which only has hardwood floors. He is inspired by the bright colors and lots of shapes on the mat."
Cribs tend to be one of the pricier baby must-haves, but there are plenty of affordable cribs on the market, including this one from DaVinci. Crafted from solid, sturdy pine, the Jenny Lind crib base has four height options that can be adjusted as your baby grows. And once they’re a little older, you can purchase a conversion kit to transition this to a toddler bed and daybed.
This tub can be used from the newborn years to toddlerhood, stretching your dollar even further. You start with the mesh sling that gently cradles and secures your baby before moving on to the insert to keep her from sliding. That same insert is used to provide stability as they master sitting, and can be removed completely once they gain full balance and body control. The tub comes with a squeeze bottle and whale-shaped scoop to help you wash and even entertain baby during bath time. There’s also a hook so you can hang it for quick drying and easy storing.
Sure, the Tripp Trapp by Stokke isn’t cheap at face value. But since the chair is designed to be used from babyhood through adulthood, it’s a worthwhile investment. The high chair features a machine-washable five-point harness that naturally supports the spine and encourages proper posture. As your child grows, you can remove the harness and adjust the seat and footplate to suit their needs. You will have to purchase the tray separately, but it is still an affordable option in the long run.
Buying an entertainment center for each stage of your baby’s development can be costly. Fortunately, Fisher Price’s Sit-to-Stand activity center gives you a playmat, activity seat and play table, all of which help with gross motor and sensory development. Start with the washable play mat for tummy time before upgrading to the activity chair, which has three adjustable seat positions and a spinning seat to allow your baby to play with every toy around the seat. When your child is a toddler, you can easily replace the seat with the included race ramp for the ultimate play table.
Diaper Genie has plenty of devoted fans, and for good reason. This affordable diaper pail features a double secure odor lock system to keep odors inside. A one-handed push-button design makes it easy to toss those stinky diapers while holding your baby in the other. Although this diaper pail looks great as-is, you can also buy designer fabric sleeves to create a stand-out look.
This diaper caddy looks much more expensive than its price tag suggests. As sturdy as its plastic counterparts, but more aesthetically pleasing, this cotton caddy has plenty of room to store all the diapering essentials. It also features a divider that can be adjusted to suit your needs, and even removed all together so you can use it for storage once your little one is out of diapers.
Over-the-door organizers cost significantly less than typical options, like shelving units and baskets, and also take up significantly less space. This option from Jay-Chi offers up five rows of storage—four rows of big pockets and one row of three small mesh pockets. Enforced with panels for extra structure and durability, this hanger can store diapers, wipes, blankets, clothes and more without bending under the weight.
Muslin blankets have so many uses: swaddle blankets, burp clothes, nursing covers, and more. Modern Baby’s blankets are lightweight but durable, and maintain their softness through multiple washes. Each two-pack comes with one solid blanket and one patterned blanket. You may have a hard time deciding between the 20+ options. Fortunately, they’re so affordable, you can buy a few.
You tend to save money on a car seat and stroller when you purchase them together, often known as a travel system. This set from Graco includes the SnugRide 35 Lite LX Infant Car Seat, a rear-facing option that’s suitable for kids between 4 and 35 pounds, as well as a stroller that can see you from the newborn phase to the toddler years. The stroller, which can be folded for easy storage, features a large storage basket to keep all your essentials handy and comes with a multi-position reclining seat that keeps baby comfortable as they ride.
The Infantino carrier frees up your hands while holding babies as little as 8 pounds up until they’re 32 pounds. It offers four different positions, based on your child’s size, and has an ergonomic seat that keeps the baby in the ideal position. You can easily adjust the seat, straps, and waist belt so you and your baby are both comfortable. There’s also adjustable head support to help cradle baby’s head and neck on the move.
The Graco Pack 'n Play Playard creates a portable, safe space for baby to play and nap. A push-button fold makes it easy to pack and store, while two locking wheels make it easy to move your playard around the house. A storage pocket is ideal for housing wipes and toys.
The HoMedics may lack the added features of pricier options, but it gets the job done when it comes to soothing your baby. The compact white noise machine plays more than just white noise—it offers the sounds of thunder, ocean, rain, summer nights, and a brook. You can leave the sound running all night or choose from three auto-off timer options. The machine can be powered through a plug in a wall outlet or batteries.
These jersey knit sheets feel like a comfy old T-shirt. They come in a two pack, so you always have another at the ready in case of blowouts. The sheets are designed for mattresses up to six inches thick and won’t shrink in the dryer. Choose from a slew of different colors and themes.
Having multiple swaddles can come in handy, especially when your baby is in a spit and poop-splosion phase. And these particular swaddles are a dream for parents, given their security and simplicity. Just place your baby inside, fold over the wings, and secure for a safe night’s rest. There’s also a foot pouch you can pull down for middle-of-the night diaper changes.
At $100, this changing table is less expensive than the competition. It features a cushy surface with a safety belt where baby can rest her head during diaper changes. A guardrail around all sides keeps your little one secure while you get to work. Two roomy storage shelves below can store diapering gear and even serve as a dresser for baby’s clothes.
Having multiple bath sets can come in handy when you’re not completely on top of laundry. And this Simple Joy’s bundle offers a lot of linens: five washcloths, two hooded towels and one regular towel. Available in four fun themes, each item is crafted from a soft cotton-polyester blend.
Ruvalino’s backpack has a simple design that makes it easy to repurpose when your diapering days are over. It features two large compartments for storing diapers, wipes, the included changing pad and more, as well as an insulated front pocket to keep bottles at the ideal temperature. There are 16 pockets throughout the bag to help you stay organized. The bag’s opening is large enough so you can easily find what you need, but not so large that it requires two hands to open.
The right baby monitor should alleviate your anxiety, not add to it, which is why we love the Nanit Plus. One of the most advanced baby monitors on the market, the Nanit Pro offers high-definition footage and two-way audio so you can have clear eyes and ears on your baby while they sleep. The monitor connects to the Nanit app, allowing you to check on your baby from anywhere. The app also tracks your baby’s sleep habits and provides custom guidance to help them get the best rest.
Parent Reviews
"I have it, I love it even though my baby isn't sleeping in her crib at night yet. Gives me good peace of mind when leaving her in there to nap or sleep." — cheriberri
"We have it. We had one issue in the beginning where it wouldn’t connect to the network but it was under a year so we got it swapped out and now it works perfectly with no issues. We don’t even use the band feature but I still love it! Video and audio quality is great. It also has like this built in sound machine so we play the wind sound throughout the night." — christinaalanis
Pumping is a true time suck (pun intended), as more traditional breast pumps must be connected to an outlet to work. Enter Willow’s wearable pump. The hospital-grade device slips right into your nursing bra, so you can express and collect milk while tackling chores around the house—or just sitting away from an outlet. Leak-proof and easy-to-assemble, the pump connects to an app that allows you to control suction levels and timing, as well as track your sessions.
Parent Reviews
"I have the Willow. It is VERY convenient and has gotten me out of numerous sticky situations. With that being said, it it doesn’t remove milk as efficiently as my Medela pump I got with insurance. Also, it holds max 4oz so if you pump more than that, you have to change the bags. And there is a "flip to finish" function that needs to be done after every pump session otherwise you can lose milk." — jormrmr
A sound machine can help your baby transition from the womb, where they were exposed to constant whooshing sounds. But the Hatch Rest+ does more than play soothing sounds for your baby. The bluetooth-controlled device can be used as a multi-colored nightlight or two-way audio monitor. An additional perk is the ability to program the Hatch Rest+ to display certain colored lights and play specific sounds at designated times throughout the day. Parents of older children find this useful to help transition kids from asleep to awake, and vice versa. All of this can be done via the Hatch app on your phone.
Parent Reviews
"I’ve tried so many with my 6 and 3 yr old. By far buy it. You can set time limits due awake snd naps.which is great when they are older. The light is bright enough to do diaper changes at night but not too bright." —cmays23
"We opted for the plus because of: Alexa integration (tricky, but awesome once it's set up). I like the idea of using it hands free. We have echos and dots in almost every room, so I can change my presets without going into the app or messing with the unit. (I can walk by the bathroom onto the way to the baby's room and tell Alexa to turn on x preset for late night diaper changes or feedings when I need a different/brighter light, for example.); Backup battery (8ish hours).; On board clock; Wifi as opposed to bluetooth. I hear a lot of complaints with the range of bluetooth and as long as you have decent, reliable internet, this elimates that issue." — motherofwolves17
Most diaper pails mask the inevitable diaper odors, but the Munchkin UV Diaper Pail takes it a step further. Whenever you close the lid, four hospital-grade UV lights work to disinfect and eliminate an array of germs and bacteria. If you’re worried about potential UV exposure, rest assured the system shuts off once the lid is open. And the pail is compatible with Munchkin bags, so refilling is easy.
Parent Reviews
"This Munchkin UV is the diaper pail of my dreams! I am using it for wee wee pads for my elderly dogs. I used to bag them and tie off the bags, but they still leaked some odor. With this Munchkin, I can open the lid with my foot, put the pad in, and as I close the lid, it automatically twists the bag to seal off odor. Then the UV light goes to work killing bacteria in the top area, and I smell nothing. It may be the most expensive diaper pail on the market, but just bite the bullet and treat yourself to the best. You won't be sorry."
Parents rave over the Cybex Sirona, the first car seat to feature a 360-degree rotatable seat, which makes it easy to switch from rear- to forward-facing. It also has SensorSafe technology built into the chest clip that gives you a heads up via the app if your child unbuckles themselves, if your child’s been seated too long, if your child’s been left in the car, or if the back seat becomes too hot or cold. Cool, right?

"I have the Cybex Sirona and really like it. It’s comfortable for baby and extremely safe, which is what matters to me. Didn’t have any issues with the swivel feature. My car is not small so I can’t say anything about that but we have been using it for 4 months and are happy with it." — GreyhoundMom83
"We have the Cybex Sirona S and we absolutely LOVE it! The fact that it rotates is a game changer. It’s super easy and effortless getting baby in and out of the car seat. We are really happy with this purchase and highly recommend it." — darakay1
Many babies are soothed by a rocking motion, and will instantly scream the minute you set them down to do a chore. (Or, you know, pee.) This is where the Mamaroo comes in handy. Unlike other swings and bouncers, which require a parent’s push to keep things moving, the Mamaroo starts with just the touch of a button. The swing offers five movements—rocking, bouncing, swinging, car ride, and wave—as well as five speed options and four built-in sounds to amp up the level of soothing. And, for the super techy, you can pair the Mamaroo with your bluetooth or Alexa for even more hands-off control. As with any swing it should not be used unsupervised nor should it be used as a place for a baby to nap. If they fall asleep, make sure to transfer them to a safe sleep space.

"I’m having baby #3 and it was a huge hit with baby #1 and #2. It totally saved our sanity and was the one piece of baby gear both of them loved and would happily sit in. It has been 100% worth it for us. We’ve never purchased another type of swing and just use the Mamaroo and have a lightweight bouncer for when we shower or move around the house etc."
"Ours was a lifesaver! On a few desperate nights he slept in it right next to my bed. Like others have said it really only lasts until they start trying to roll or sit up in it. But for me it was a no brainer to get one for our second. I bought my second hand, as the full price tag seems over the top."
Warming your baby’s bottle is something you’ll have to often and, occasionally, away from your home. And sometimes hot water just isn’t enough or even accessible. Fortunately, Munchkin’s battery-powered bottle warmer makes it easy to heat breast milk or formula on the go. Compatible with most major bottle brands, including Avent and Dr. Brown and Medela with the addition adapter, the device heats your baby’s bottle to 98.6 degree in mere minutes and holds the temperature for 30 minutes.
Sleep is one thing you can almost guarantee you’ll be short on during those first few months as a parent, and during this time a warm cup of coffee just might be your saving grace. Parents rave over this temperature-controlled mug that connects to your smartphone and reheats your beverages on command. It also has an auto-sleep feature that intelligently senses when to turn on or off, and shuts off automatically after two hours of inactivity.

"As a mom and full time student coffee is a must! I got this as a gift for mother's day and I love it! I normally don't have time to finish my coffee before it's cold then it usually gets dumped. This mug keeps coffee warm so I can actually drink it!"
Your baby may be more soothed by your voice rather than white noise, but you don’t want to go hoarse. A temperature spike can be incredibly worrisome, especially if it’s your baby’s first fever. But the VAVA Smart Baby Thermometer may help put parents at ease so they can rest. The rechargeable device has a 24-hour battery life, so don’t have to worry about it dying in the middle of the night.

Not only is this stroller sleek and stylish, but it also assists on your walks. There are many household tasks that may be difficult to do now that you have a baby in tow. The Roomba eliminates one of those: vacuuming. Made up of three different compartments with interchangeable lids that click together for easy stacking and packing, Skip Hop’s container can be used to store snacks once your baby has moved past the bottle phase. Heck, you can even store your own snacks to prevent travel-induced hanger.

"This was a great buy for the price and how many pieces come with it. It’s the ultimate travel kit to have without having to pack individual containers. My favorite price is the stackable. They are so cool and you can mix them too! It’s great for any age child it grows with them. I sometimes put graham crackers at the top, fresh fruit in the middle and water at the bottom especially for on the go days or even just running to the store. It’s travel snacks on hand."
When you’re on the go, you might not always have access to a bottle warmer or even water that’s hot enough to heat your baby’s bottles within minutes. Tommee Tippee offers parents the perfect option with this pint-sized, thermal bottle warmer that works without any batteries or cords. The water inside was SCALDING still from the kettle. . . 10/10 recommend, just make sure the lid is screwed on tight and test for leakage with each use!!!"
Navigating an airport or new city is a lot easier with two free hands. The Líllé baby carrier not only makes that possible, but also easy. But after a few wears mine is comfortably soft! You can even ‘wear’ it before the baby comes if you want to break it in. Any carrier with mesh and even some without will need to be broken in to be soft. And lillebaby customer service is great!"
A crib may seem like a hefty item to travel with, but this fold-up travel version by BabyBjörn will convince you otherwise. Featuring an airy mesh fabric on all four sides for optimal breathability, the BabyBjörn crib folds flat in seconds and can be stored in a compact carrier bag. Weighing less than two pounds, this automatic pop-up tent is the perfect accessory for any vacation that takes you outdoors. It’s easy to pack in your travel bag and can be set up in minutes. title: My Child Doesn't Nap At Home | ZERO TO THREE
text:
My Child Doesn’t Nap At Home
It can be tough to have a toddler up all day, especially one who is cranky and overtired. And no naps mean no breaks for Mom and Dad. It can also feel pretty frustrating for parents to know that their child is a dream at child care but won’t go down without a fight on the weekend. The comforting news is that this dilemma is pretty common. Here’s why.
First, child-care providers are dealing with children in groups, so there is a greater need for rules and cooperation than there is at home. And children are amazingly adaptable. I remember my own surprise at learning that my 2-year-old, who hadn’t napped since she was 15 months, played quietly with toys and books for an entire hour-and-a-half most days at child care and sometimes actually fell asleep!
The first step is to know that you are being good parents by helping your child get the sleep he needs, even if he cries and complains. Keeping this in mind will help you follow through on a plan.
Rebecca Parlakian
Second, there is a difference in the nature of the adult-child relationships in childcare versus home. A childcare provider may care deeply about your son but she does not have the same emotional connection to him as you do. This is why parents almost always find it harder than care providers to set and enforce limits. Parents have a tendency to get love and limits mixed up, feeling they are doing something wrong by setting a limit their child protests. For working parents, enforcing a naptime can be an especially tough limit to set because it means another separation from their child (if only for an hour or two) and loud, unhappy protests.
The first step is to know that you are being good parents by helping your child get the sleep he needs, even if he cries and complains. Keeping this in mind will help you follow through on a plan. Start by talking with your childcare provider to learn how she helps the children transition to naptime. Is there lunch, then diaper changing, then a story? Does she rub your son’s back? Dim the lighting? Is there music or any other ambient noises? Try to re-create the atmosphere and rituals as much as possible at home. Also keep in mind that a real nap-killer on the weekends is changing your son’s routine. Falling asleep in the car for 15 minutes here and there (as many kids do while parents run errands) means he may not nap when you get home. And if you let your son sleep later on a Saturday or Sunday morning, he may not go down at naptime. So try to keep to your child’s usual schedule as best as you can.
When you put your son down for a nap, put a few soft toys or padded books in his crib. Some toddlers need time to wind down or, after napping, time to wake up; quiet play can often do the trick. (This is not recommended for babies under 12 months for whom soft objects in the crib can be a suffocation hazard.)
If your son cries, go in to comfort him briefly—but don’t linger or take him out of the crib. Give him a cuddle and explain, It’s naptime now. You can decide if you’d like to go back in at subsequent intervals (say, 5 minutes or 10 minutes later if he’s still crying) or not at all. The approach you choose depends on your baby’s temperament and what you feel might work best for him. The going-in-periodically-to-soothe routine not only didn’t calm my 9-month-old son, it confused him, made him angrier, and prolonged the protesting.
Remember, this is a learning process and takes time.
Rebecca Parlakian
Remember, this is a learning process and takes time. Start out with a half-hour as a goal. Put him down, go back in as you’d like. Then, if he doesn’t fall asleep, go get him after a half-hour. Wait a few days, then shoot for 45 minutes, then an hour. Soon you may find that he is learning to fall asleep on his own.
The most important thing is consistency. Going in and picking him up one day, then letting him cry it out the next is not likely to work and will only confuse your toddler. When you are consistent with his napping ritual, he will learn to adapt more easily and quickly.
preview:
My Child Doesn’t Nap At Home
It can be tough to have a toddler up all day, especially one who is cranky and overtired. And no naps mean no breaks for Mom and Dad.

title: My Child Keeps Waking Up At Night | ZERO TO THREE
text:
My Child Keeps Waking Up At Night
A. I remember those dead-of-night wake-ups so well—my son, 6-months-old, crying it out at 3:30 a.m., not at all soothed by my whispered “night-night, time to go to sleep now.” If listening to him cry himself to sleep wasn’t bad enough, then hearing my 3-year-old call out “Mommy?” really made it a tough (and early) morning.
Explain how her brother is learning how to sleep through the night. Remind her that she knows how to get herself back to sleep when she wakes up. And make a plan together for what she can do if she’s awakened by her brother.
Rebecca Parlakian
Here are some ideas to help you make it through sleep training as well rested as possible:
Make noise—white noise, that is. The sounds will drown out middle-of-the-night crying for your older child. You can buy a white-noise machine, but I always ran a room fan in my older child’s room. It provided just enough “noise” to cover the cries coming from her little brother’s room. Other families I know play a relaxation CD softly on repeat.
Talk to your daughter about what’s going on. Explain how her brother is learning how to sleep through the night. Remind her that she knows how to get herself back to sleep when she wakes up. And make a plan together for what she can do if she’s awakened by her brother. For instance, you could tell her: “Close your eyes again, cuddle with your bear, and think about the story we read right before bedtime.”
Don’t linger. If your daughter wakes you up because her brother woke her, go in and explain (in whispers) what is happening: “Your brother is crying because he’s having trouble falling back asleep.” Don’t turn on any lights or do anything that may make her more alert. Let her know what the plan is, and be consistent each time she gets up. You could say, “Mommy is going to tuck you in now and give you a kiss. Then we all have to go to bed until morning.”
Be patient. You may have not one, but two cranky children for a while until your youngest is sleeping through the night. When you see more frequent tantrums, whining, or clinginess, you’ll know why. Just think how tough it is for you to make it through the day—and you’re a grown-up who can order a latte! So hang in there, maintain a regular bedtime and naptime for your kids, and someday soon everybody in your home will be sleeping tight.

title: My Husband Won’t Pick Up or Comfort our 12-Month-Old Because he Says it Will Make Him Soft | ZERO TO THREE
text:
My Husband Won’t Pick Up or Comfort our 12-Month-Old Because he Says it Will Make Him Soft
You raise two important issues: how to respond to a toddler who is upset and establishing a way for you and your husband to effectively communicate and resolve child-rearing challenges when you have different ideas about them.
The first issue to think about is this idea of raising a child to be “soft.” I am assuming that for your husband, his concern is that comforting your son will not make him independent and able to handle challenges—that he will always look for and expect someone else to help him when is upset. Your husband is not alone in his beliefs. Many dads, and moms too, share this concern. These ideas often come from the messages parents received from their families as they were growing up. And of course, many come from a person’s ideas about gender—that for a boy to be “tough,” he shouldn’t cry or need help when upset.
A much more useful approach is for each partner to start by looking at the big picture. You both want your son to grow up healthy and strong. You both have his best interest at heart. Neither of you want to harm him. You simply have different ideas about what is going to help him reach this goal.
Rebecca Parlakian
I am also assuming your worry is that by not comforting him, your son may feel insecure and less trusting of you, which in the end may be what actually makes him “soft.”
What tends to happen in situations in which parents disagree is that they get polarized. Both argue their points and the more they defend their position, the more extreme a stance each needs to take in order to make their point.
A much more useful approach is for each partner to start by looking at the big picture. You both want your son to grow up healthy and strong. You both have his best interest at heart. Neither of you want to harm him. You simply have different ideas about what is going to help him reach this goal.
Next, each of you clearly articulates what you think and feel about the situation and why, while the other listens without interrupting. As you listen to each other, think about what your partner is saying that makes sense to you and validate that. In this case, you might validate not your husband’s behavior, but his underlying intention—to help make your son a competent, independent person. It would also be helpful to point out some things your husband does with your son that help him thrive. Having been heard, and also complimented, your husband is much more likely to be open to hearing your thoughts and feelings.
Children are not born with the skill of managing their own strong emotions. In fact, young children ultimately learn to comfort and soothe themselves by having the adults closest to them comfort and soothe them. When parents and caregivers help children calm down when they are sad, scared, angry, or overwhelmed, children are better able to manage their own feelings as they grow.
Children are not born with the skill of managing their own strong emotions. In fact, young children ultimately learn to comfort and soothe themselves by having the adults closest to them comfort and soothe them.
Rebecca Parlakian
The response that would most benefit your son actually takes into account both your and your husband’s beliefs and values about child rearing. Here’s how it might look in “real-life.” Your son falls down as he is playing a chasing game with you. He starts to cry.
- You or your husband say something like, “Uh oh! You fell down.” (Use a loving, but matter-of-fact voice, not one that is overly concerned or panicky because kids pick up on their parents’ cues about how to feel or react to any given situation.)
- Provide some physical comfort, a hug, or a gentle stroke to the affected area of his body.
- Encourage him to play again, letting him know you think he can do it.
By combining your approaches, you and your husband can team up in this extreme sport we call parenting.

title: My Toddler Is Afraid of the Dark. What Can I Do to Help?
text:
Your toddler never used to mind hitting the sack with the lights off. But all of a sudden, she wants you to keep the lights on in her bedroom — or she wakes in the middle of the night and is afraid of falling back asleep on her own.
Things that go bump in the night are a normal part of everyday life to adults, but to toddlers they can be downright terrifying.
Why is my toddler suddenly afraid of the dark?
Once a toddler’s thought process becomes more sophisticated, fears can appear to make kids anxious about a whole array of stuff — especially at night, when it’s easy for anyone’s imagination to run wild.
In addition to a rich fantasy life, toddlers are more aware of the larger world and its dangers. They have longer memories than babies do, so a negative experience (like falling off a swing) may trigger a longer-lasting fear.
Toddlers are also highly suggestible: Your little one might assume, for example, that the monster hiding in the girl’s bedroom in the book you read last night could show up in her room as well. Or, if a sibling is afraid of the dark, your little one might decide that’s enough reason for her to be afraid too.
Changes (a move to a new home, the arrival of a new sibling) can also cause a child to feel insecure and afraid.
When does a fear of the dark typically start and end?
Children are most often afraid of the dark starting at around the age of about 2 through the preschool years, although it can appear in older children as well. A fear of the dark usually lasts for a few weeks to a few months.
What can you do about your toddler's fear of the dark?
A few strategies can help keep your toddler’s imagination in check and help her to self-soothe and feel secure alone at night:
Acknowledge the fear
While a fear of the dark may seem irrational to you, it’s very real to your toddler — so ignoring it won’t make it go away. In fact, ignoring or playfully teasing a fear can magnify it.
Instead, let your child talk about her dread ("Tell me what's scaring you") without denying her feelings (don't say: "Big girls aren't afraid of the dark"). Be reassuring ("I'll be close by if you need me") without making too much of the fear or overdoing the attention.
Stay in bed and keep the lights off
When you heed your child's calls in the middle of the night, don't reach for the light or bring her to your bed. To help her learn to overcome her fears, have her stay in her own bed and comfort her in the dark.
If your little one just can’t fall back asleep without you, it’s better for you to sleep in her room than for her to sleep in yours. Just try to avoid sleeping in the same room too often, since that might encourage your little one to become dependent on you to sleep.
Play in the dark
Try to make fun associations with the dark by having a treasure hunt for glow-in-the-dark objects or hunting for fireflies in the backyard.
Try relaxation training
Before bedtime, close your eyes in the dark together and imagine a relaxing scene, like playing at the beach.
Offer a security object
As long as your child is over 1 year old (so as not to risk sudden infant death syndrome, or SIDS), give her a security object like a favorite stuffed animal, for example, to help her feel more secure and relaxed throughout the night.
Leave a little light on
A night light can go a long way toward melting a child's fears. Choose one no brighter than 4 and 7 watts, and look for a yellow- or amber-colored light bulb, avoiding white- or blue-colored light.
Or consider leaving her door slightly ajar and the hall light on. She may be comforted by the sliver of light and household noises.
Check on her
Tell your little one you’re right next door, then pop your head in every five to 10 minutes until she falls asleep if she’s anxious about you leaving her room.
Work with her imagination
If she's convinced there's a troll in her closet, do a search. You can also appoint a favorite toy to stand guard, or teach your child a special word to ward off trolls.
You can also arm her with a lucky charm like a flashlight, magic wand or spray bottle filled with anti-troll potion.
Applaud bravery
Make a big deal when your child confronts her fear — no matter how small the step.
You might even want to create a star system to award your child for being brave on her own if she stays in her bed with the lights off all night long. A mini celebration or a tiny treat may well be in order.
How can you prevent a fear of the dark?
To help prevent a fear of the dark, steer clear of scary TV shows, movies and books. And avoid threats that can have a toddler’s imagination running wild ("If you don't behave, the troll will get you").
Heads up: Teasing or employing logic ("See, your room looks the same with the lights turned on or off") can have the opposite effect. These tactics may make her more fearful because she'll think you don't understand the danger she believes is very real.
Plus, teasing can zap a child's self-esteem. Instead, reassure your toddler that she's safe, even in the dark.
preview: Your toddler never used to mind hitting the sack with the lights off. But all of a sudden, she wants you to keep the lights on in her bedroom — or she wakes in the middle of the night and is afraid of falling back asleep on her own.

title: My baby is fussy! Is something wrong? - KellyMom.com
text:
Whether breastfed or formula fed, during their first few months, many babies have a regular fussy period, which usually occurs in the late afternoon or evening. Some babies’ fussy periods come so regularly that parents can set their clocks by it! The standard infant fussiness usually starts at about 2 to 3 weeks, peaks at 6 weeks and is gone by 3 to 4 months. It lasts on “average” 2 to 4 hours per day. Of course, there is a wide variety of normal.
To distinguish between “normal” and a problem, normal usually occurs around the same time of day, with approximately the same intensity (with some variation); responds to some of the same things each time, such as motion, holding, frequent breastfeeding, etc.; and occurs in a baby who has other times of the day that he is contentedly awake or asleep. Normal fussiness tends to occur during the time of the day that the baby usually stays awake more, the most common time is in the evening right before the time that the baby takes his longest stretch of sleep.

If you feel that your baby’s fussiness is not normal, it’s never a bad idea to get baby checked by the doctor to rule out any illness. A common cause of fussy, colic-like symptoms in babies is foremilk-hindmilk imbalance (also called oversupply syndrome, too much milk, etc.) and/or forceful let-down. Other causes of fussiness in babies include diaper rash, thrush, food sensitivities, nipple confusion, low milk supply, etc.
Babies normally fuss for many reasons: overtiredness, overstimulation, loneliness, discomfort, etc. Babies are often very fussy when they are going through growth spurts. Do know that it is normal for you to be “beside yourself” when your baby cries: you actually have a hormonal response that makes you feel uncomfortable when your baby cries.

|
One of the most interesting things I’ve seen in the research regarding infant fussiness is that almost anything a parent tries to reduce fussiness will work, but only for a short time (a few days), and then other strategies need to be used.
If you nurse and it doesn’t seem to help, then try other comfort measures. If you pick him up or nurse him, and baby is content, then that was what he needed. If it works, use it!
Your baby will not be spoiled if you hold him and nurse him often – quite the opposite, in fact. Studies have shown that when babies are held often and responded to quickly, the babies cry less, and the parents learn to read baby’s cues more quickly. A young child’s need for his mother is very intense – as intense as his need for food. Know that your child really needs you. It is not about manipulation or something you can “fix” with the right discipline. Often a baby who is perceived as fussy is simply a baby who needs more contact with mom (and is smart enough to express this need) and is content once his needs are met. See the links below to read more about spoiling.
Caring for a fussy baby can be very stressful! Give both yourself and baby some extra TLC. Surround yourself with supportive people, de-stress in other areas if possible (for example, minimize housework), and tell yourself you are doing a great job. It is very difficult to feel good about yourself as a parent when you have a fussy baby. Don’t be too alarmed if your efforts seem to have no positive effect – they are. When you stay with your baby to try to provide comfort you are beginning to teach your baby that he can count on you and that he is loved.

title: My baby is sick – should I continue to breastfeed? - KellyMom.com
text:
Absolutely! Anytime a sick baby is able to take anything by mouth, it should be his mother’s milk. Mom’s milk provides antibodies specifically tailored to fight baby’s illness, and is quickly and easily digested.
Temporary weaning, besides depriving baby of antibodies to fight the illness and the most easily digestible food source available, can make life miserable for both mother and baby. Nursing is extremely comforting to an ill baby and a major part of the healing process. Mom would have to pump to maintain milk supply and then there would still be some risk that supply would drop somewhat. Not pumping would put mom at risk for breast infection and extreme discomfort.
Now infants can get
all their vitamin D
from their mothers’ milk;
no drops needed with
our sponsor's
TheraNatal Lactation Complete
by THERALOGIX. Use PRC code “KELLY” for a special discount!
- Colds and congestion
- Baby refuses to nurse when sick
- Vomiting and Diarrhea
- Breastmilk vs. Pedialyte
- Additional Information and References
If baby has a cold and is congested, it can make breastfeeding difficult. However, it is almost always easier for a sick baby to nurse than to take a bottle. If your baby has a stuffy nose and is having a hard time breathing and nursing at the same time, try the following:
- Keep baby as upright as possible while nursing. At night, try propping up on lots of pillows and nursing/sleeping semi-upright. Also try the Australian position (mom is “down under”) – in this position, mom is lying on her back and baby is on top (facing down), tummy to tummy with mom.
- The best thing you can do to help baby’s illness end quickly is to nurse often – that way she gets lots of the antibodies that your body is making to help her fight off the illness. Frequent nursing also helps to ensure that baby is getting plenty of milk (congested babies often nurse for shorter times since it’s hard to breathe and nurse at the same time).
- Use saline drops (or breastmilk) & a rubber suction bulb to clear baby’s nose before nursing (if baby won’t tolerate the bulb syringe, then the saline drops/breastmilk alone should still help).
- Put baby on your knees, face up and tilt your knees a little downhill (so baby’s head is angled away from you, toward the floor).
- Put 2-3 drops of saline in each nostril and let it sit for a minute or so.
- To suction the mucus out, squeeze the bulb part of the syringe first, gently stick the rubber tip into one nostril, then slowly release the bulb.
- If baby is really congested, you may have to do this several times a day. Do it BEFORE baby nurses; if you do it afterwards your baby may spit up everything she’s eaten because syringing can stimulate the gag reflex.
- To prepare saline drops at home, dissolve one teaspoon of salt in two cups of warm water.
- Run a vaporizer or humidifier, preferably in a small closed room.
- Boil a pot of water (some moms use small crock pots/potpourri pots), remove from the stove and add a few drops of essential oil (for example, eucalyptus, sage or balsam), and let the scent permeate the air. This may help relieve some head congestion.
- Do NOT apply products containing peppermint oil, camphor or menthol on the face (especially in the nose) or chest of a baby or young child. There have been cases where the direct application of menthol or camphor products (for example, Vicks VapoRub™) to baby’s skin resulted in severe breathing difficulties or liver problems (see Camphor Hepatoxicity, Camphor Monograph and Menthol Toxicology).
- Nurse in a steamy bathroom. To pump up the steam, run a really hot shower and set a chair outside the shower for nursing.
- The US Food and Drug Administration recommends against giving over the counter cold and cough medicines to children under 2.
- The Consumer Healthcare Products Association recommends over-the-counter cough and cold products not be used in children less than 4 years old due to the risk of serious and life-threatening side effects. There is also no evidence that over-the-counter cold meds actually benefit children younger than six years old. There are several well controlled studies where there has been no difference shown between children given medication and those who have not.
Sometimes moms are advised to limit or discontinue breastfeeding because milk increases mucus production. This is not good advice for two reasons:
- You are not a cow and your milk is not a dairy product. So even if dairy is a problem, your milk would not be.
- In addition, there is no scientific evidence that cow’s milk results in the production of more mucus unless you are allergic to dairy products.
Some babies may refuse to nurse when sick (particularly if something like a sore throat or pressure from an ear infection makes nursing painful for baby). If this happens, try different nursing positions – particularly those where baby is upright – and keep offering to nurse at least every hour or so. See also: What to do about a nursing strike. Rest assured that your baby will return to nursing when he feels better. Here are some things to try if baby is too uncomfortable to nurse:
- feed baby expressed milk from a cup, dropper, spoon or syringe
- try momsicles, or freeze your milk until it’s slushy and let baby eat it with a spoon
- for older babies (over six months) who are eating solids: if baby will take solids but not nurse, add lots of breastmilk to the solids. You might also try making yogurt from breastmilk.
Another time when moms can be unsure about whether to continue nursing is when baby has diarrhea or is vomiting. Frequent and loose stools in a breastfed baby is not necessarily diarrhea. Here is more information on normal stooling patterns for breastfed babies: What Are Baby’s Stools Supposed to Look Like? Diarrhea in a breastfed baby is diagnosed when a baby has 12-16 stools per day (or more often than the baby’s regular stool frequency), watery stools, and an offensive odor to the stools. Just one of these symptoms does not in itself mean the baby has diarrhea.
The current recommendations when vomiting or diarrhea is present in the breastfed child are as follows:
- Breastfeeding should be the FIRST choice if your child can take anything by mouth. Because of the ease and rapidity with which breastmilk is digested, even if your child vomits or stools shortly after nursing, he will still have retained some of the nutrients. Other foods that are often suggested (such as Pedialyte, sports drinks, gelatins and sodas) offer little nutritional value and none of the antibodies that human milk contains.
- When your breastfed child is ill you’ll want to offer more frequent feedings — this can limit the volume taken in at one time and helps to comfort and soothe a sick child. If your child is vomiting often and not keeping the milk down for long, it may be helpful to breastfeed frequently but limit the length of each nursing session (so your child takes in less milk at once). Another option is for Mom to express some milk before breastfeeding so that the milk flow is slower. RARELY does the baby who is allowed to breastfeed at will during a vomiting or diarrhea illness become dehydrated.
The use of an oral rehydration therapy such as Pedialyte is a sound recommendation for a formula-fed infant who is vomiting or who has diarrhea, but using this in place of breastmilk offers no benefit to the breastfed baby. Human milk is a natural fluid, unlike formula and other milk products, that again is easily and rapidly digested.
Forego the Pedialyte as long as baby continues to nurse well and as long as there are no signs of dehydration. If baby is showing signs of dehydration, talk to your doctor. Following are signs of dehydration:
- fewer than 2 wet diapers in a 24-hour period
- baby not behaving normally (more irritable, less active, sleeping more)
- lethargy
- listlessness
- weak cry
- no tears (in an infant older than 3 months)
- dry mouth
- skin that stays wrinkly-looking when pinched (pull up the skin on the front of the hand; it should pop easily back into place – not stay pinched or wrinkly-looking)
- eyes that look sunken
- cool, clammy extremities, especially the fingers and toes
- fast breathing, or a heart that is beating faster than usual
- fever
Babies who are allowed to continue breastfeeding through an illness such as this are less likely to become dehydrated than those who are taken off the breast. Continued nursing also provides your child with precious antibodies that will prevent the illness from worsening and speed healing. Nursing is also very comforting to your child, which is healing in itself.
Breastfed babies sometimes need oral rehydration therapy (Pedialyte, etc), though far less often than artificially fed infants. The World Health Organization recommends continuing to breastfeed during and after oral rehydration therapy. Research shows that babies lose more weight and actually have more stools during diarrhea when they are deprived of breastmilk.
Sometimes moms are recommended to withhold breastmilk “because it is a dairy product.” Breastmilk is NOT considered a dairy or milk product (mom is not a cow!).
Anytime there is diarrhea (in adults as well as babies) it can take a while for the bowel to heal and the stools to get back to normal. So even if you identify the problem and take corrective action, you may not see favorable results for a few weeks. This is because if the bowel gets irritated it is harder for the bowel to digest lactose – the undigested lactose then actually creates more irritation and runny stools… it can be tough to turn around at times. There is usually an inflammatory response with diarrhea – that is the part that takes time to heal. When the cause of the problem is removed (when baby recovers from the illness), the gut will heal even if the baby is still fed breastmilk. More here on secondary lactose intolerance.
When baby is sick, moms are sometimes told to discontinue or restrict breastfeeding and substitute an oral rehydration therapy such as Pedialyte. This outdated practice has been shown to offer no benefits to the breastfed baby, and can even delay healing.
Your milk has four things that your baby needs even more than usual when he’s sick:
- Antibodies to fight this illness – you want baby to get as much of these as possible. Encourage *more* nursing rather than less. Pedialyte has no antibodies.
- Liquids to keep baby hydrated. Your baby may not be eating as much because he doesn’t feel well. Sick babies are more likely to nurse than to take anything else by mouth, so nursing is important to keep baby hydrated. Keeping baby well hydrated also helps keep the mucus secretions thinned out if baby has a cold or other congestion. So again, you want to nurse *more*. Pedialyte will keep baby hydrated, but so will breastmilk.
- Concentrated nutrients. Breastmilk is easily and quickly digested, so baby gets more nutrients and absorbs them faster. Pedialyte will keep baby hydrated but has little nutritional value. Again, it’s best to nurse *more* since baby may not be eating as much if he feels bad.
- Comfort. Sick babies need more comforting – what better way to do this than at the breast?
See the section above for more information on the use of Pedialyte in breastfed babies – Breastfed babies sometimes do need oral rehydration therapy (Pedialyte, etc), though far less often than artificially fed infants. The World Health Organization recommends continuing to breastfeed during and after oral rehydration therapy.
